text: LESSON 1: SEPARATING BUSINESS AND PERSONAL MONEY. This is the #1 mistake young entrepreneurs make. If you mix business money with personal money, you will never know if your business is actually profitable. Solution: Get TWO mobile money accounts — one for business, one for personal. Every sale goes into the business account. Pay yourself a fixed 'salary' each month from business to personal. Even if it's just UGX 200,000, this discipline is what separates successful business owners from those who fail. Example: If you sell rolexes and earn UGX 3,000,000/month but spend it all on personal things, your business has zero savings for emergencies or growth.

LESSON 2: UNDERSTANDING PROFIT vs REVENUE. Revenue (Sales) is ALL the money that comes in. Profit is what remains AFTER paying ALL expenses. Many young entrepreneurs think they're making profit when they're actually losing money. Example: You sell 100 chickens for UGX 22,000 each = UGX 2,200,000 REVENUE. But you spent: Chicks UGX 400,000 + Feed UGX 800,000 + Vaccines UGX 50,000 + Rent UGX 100,000 + Transport UGX 50,000 + Your labor (yes, your time has value!) UGX 200,000 = UGX 1,600,000 EXPENSES. Your PROFIT = UGX 2,200,000 - UGX 1,600,000 = UGX 600,000. That's your REAL profit. If you thought you made UGX 2,200,000, you would spend more than you earned.

LESSON 3: SIMPLE RECORD-KEEPING. You don't need an accountant or a computer. You need ONE notebook with TWO sections: MONEY IN and MONEY OUT. Write EVERY transaction, every single day. Date | Description | Amount In | Amount Out. Example entries: April 1 | Sold 5 trays eggs | 50,000 | — | April 1 | Bought 2 bags feed | — | 250,000 | April 1 | Transport to market | — | 10,000. At the end of each week, add up Money In and Money Out. If Money In > Money Out, you're profitable that week. If not, look at where the money went and find ways to reduce costs or increase sales. This simple habit is what makes the difference between a business that grows and one that dies.

LESSON 4: PRICING FOR PROFIT. Many youth set prices too low because they're afraid of losing customers. But if your price doesn't cover costs + a reasonable profit, you're working for free — or worse, losing money. Formula: Selling Price = (Cost of materials + Labor + Overhead) × 1.3 to 1.5. The 1.3-1.5 multiplier gives you a 30-50% markup, which covers unexpected costs and gives profit. Example: Making one rolex costs you UGX 1,200 (ingredients + charcoal + your time). Minimum selling price: UGX 1,200 × 1.5 = UGX 1,800. If the market price is UGX 2,500, you have a healthy UGX 1,300 profit per rolex. If competitors are selling at UGX 1,500, don't match them — compete on quality and service instead.

LESSON 5: SAVING AND REINVESTING. Rule of thumb for young business owners: Save at least 20% of your monthly profit. Split savings into: 1) Emergency fund (10%) — for unexpected problems (sickness, theft, equipment breakdown). Keep this in mobile money savings or SACCO. 2) Growth fund (10%) — for expanding your business (more stock, better equipment, second location). NEVER take all your profit as personal income. The businesses that grow are the ones where the owner reinvests. Example: Sarah from Mukono saves UGX 240,000/month from her UGX 1,200,000 poultry profit. After 6 months, she had UGX 1,440,000 saved — enough to add another 100 birds. Her profit grew from UGX 1,200,000 to UGX 1,800,000/month. That's the power of reinvestment.

LESSON 6: BORROWING WISELY. Not all loans are good. Before borrowing, ask: 1) Can I afford the monthly repayment from my current profit? 2) Will this loan INCREASE my income? (Good: buying more stock. Bad: buying a new phone.) 3) What's the TRUE interest rate? Some lenders advertise '10% per month' — that's 120% per year! Safer borrowing options for youth: VSLA/village savings groups (lowest interest, peer accountability), SACCOs (reasonable rates, usually 2-3% monthly), microfinance institutions (higher rates but regulated). AVOID: loan sharks, unregistered money lenders, borrowing to pay another loan. Golden rule: Only borrow what you can repay in 3-6 months from business profits alone.
